description: Complete guide to smart pointers: unique_ptr, shared_ptr, weak_ptr. Includes ownership semantics, custom deleters, make functions, and common pitfalls. Patient explanations.

sections.title: Why Smart Pointers?
sections.definition: Smart pointers automate memory management by automatically deleting the object when no longer needed. They help prevent memory leaks, double deletes, and dangling pointers.

sections.title: unique_ptr (Exclusive Ownership)
sections.definition: std::unique_ptr owns the object exclusively. Cannot be copied, only moved. When the unique_ptr is destroyed, the object is deleted. Best for most single-owner cases.

sections.title: make_unique (C++14)
sections.definition: Prefer make_unique over direct new. It's safer (exception-safe) and more concise. Available since C++14.

sections.title: shared_ptr (Shared Ownership)
sections.definition: std::shared_ptr allows multiple pointers to own the same object. A reference count tracks how many shared_ptrs point to it. When the last one is destroyed, the object is deleted.

sections.title: make_shared (Preferred for shared_ptr)
sections.definition: make_shared allocates the control block and object in a single memory allocation, more efficient and safer than using new with shared_ptr.

sections.title: weak_ptr (Breaking Cycles)
sections.definition: weak_ptr holds a non-owning reference to an object managed by shared_ptr. It doesn't affect reference count. Used to break circular dependencies that cause memory leaks.

sections.title: Circular Reference Problem and Solution
sections.definition: When two shared_ptrs point to each other, reference counts never drop to zero, causing memory leak. weak_ptr breaks the cycle.

sections.title: Custom Deleters
sections.definition: Smart pointers can use custom deleters for special cleanup (e.g., closing file handles, freeing C malloc memory, deleting arrays with unique_ptr).

sections.title: Smart Pointers in Containers
sections.definition: Store smart pointers in standard containers (vector, map, etc.) for automatic memory management.

sections.title: When to Use Which Smart Pointer
sections.definition: Guidelines: unique_ptr for exclusive ownership (most cases). shared_ptr when multiple owners needed. weak_ptr to break cycles. Prefer make_* functions.
